coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-assembled membranes with intrinsic functionalized nanochannels for high-performance nanofiltration
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Yuqing
Shen, Qin
Kawabata, Yuki
Segawa, Jumpei
Cao, Xingzhong
Guan, Kecheng
Istirokhatun, Titik
Yoshioka, Tomohisa
Matsuyama, Hideto
coredata.dc:description: Recent innovations highlight the great potential of zero-dimensional materials of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as attractive candidates for fabricating advanced nanofiltration membranes. In this study, a novel class of thin-film composite (TFC) nanofiltration membranes derived from assembled GQDs with amino/sulfonic modification (GQDs_N/S) as building blocks directly, is developed to achieve highly selective water transport for high-performance nanofiltration via interfacial polymerization. Based on the transport behavior, the intrinsically hydrophilic sulfonic groups are introduced to endow the membrane with stronger internal polarity and more accessible sites for easier water molecules infiltration, while engineering the nanochannels by covalently immobilizing GQDs between the terminal amino groups and trimesoyl chloride. Furthermore, an enhanced interparticle space with enlarged free volumes between GQDs is obtained for rapidly transporting water molecules inside the membrane, owing to the intercalation of sulfonic groups acting as spacers to break up the tightly stacking structure of GQDs. The resultant GQDs_N/S TFC membrane exhibits superior separation properties with high water permeance of 9.82 L m-2h−1 bar−1 and 97.4% rejection against Na2SO4, giving rise to more than 2-fold higher water permeability without obviously sacrificing the membrane selectivity, and revealing an outperformed separation property when compared to state-of-the-art nanofiltration membranes. Simultaneously, the sulfonic and amino sites firmly anchored on GQDs with custom-tailored functionality further enables the GQDs_N/S TFC membrane to perform an outstanding antifouling property and durability over the long-term operation condition. The proposed approach opens new pathways to fabricate highly permeable-selective nanofiltration membranes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recent innovations highlight the great potential of zero-dimensional materials of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as attractive candidates for fabricating advanced nanofiltration membranes. In this study, a novel class of thin-film composite (TFC) nanofiltration membranes derived from assembled GQDs with amino/sulfonic modification (GQDs_N/S) as building blocks directly, is developed to achieve highly selective water transport for high-performance nanofiltration via interfacial polymerization. Based on the transport behavior, the intrinsically hydrophilic sulfonic groups are introduced to endow the membrane with stronger internal polarity and more accessible sites for easier water molecules infiltration, while engineering the nanochannels by covalently immobilizing GQDs between the terminal amino groups and trimesoyl chloride. Furthermore, an enhanced interparticle space with enlarged free volumes between GQDs is obtained for rapidly transporting water molecules inside the membrane, owing to the intercalation of sulfonic groups acting as spacers to break up the tightly stacking structure of GQDs. The resultant GQDs_N/S TFC membrane exhibits superior separation properties with high water permeance of 9.82 L m-2h−1 bar−1 and 97.4% rejection against Na2SO4, giving rise to more than 2-fold higher water permeability without obviously sacrificing the membrane selectivity, and revealing an outperformed separation property when compared to state-of-the-art nanofiltration membranes. Simultaneously, the sulfonic and amino sites firmly anchored on GQDs with custom-tailored functionality further enables the GQDs_N/S TFC membrane to perform an outstanding antifouling property and durability over the long-term operation condition. The proposed approach opens new pathways to fabricate highly permeable-selective nanofiltration membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: In light of the historical trends indicating the rapid expansion of industries with increased water pollution and water consumption, water scarcity has become one of the most severe environmental challenges worldwide [1]. Membrane-based technologies provide considerably simpler, more economical, and more efficient separation for numerous water purification applications when compared to conventional technologies [2]. Although advanced membranes have been developed from several classes of organic/inorganic, and composite materials, a long-standing goal is to pursue ideally efficient desalination membrane materials that have the advantages of high permeating flux and rejection, low fouling tendency, and excellent stability [3,4].

Nanofiltration-based thin-film composite (TFC) membrane is a mature and energy-efficient technology for separating multivalent salts or small molecules within the molecular weight (Mw) of 200 ~ 1000 Da from liquid solvents [5]. The most commonly used polyamide (PA) nanofiltration membranes are primarily prepared through interfacial polymerization on a highly porous substrate [6]. In the conventional interfacial polymerization process, the amine monomers in an aqueous solution generally diffuse into an organic solvent containing trimesoyl chloride (TMC) with vigorous stirring, thus forming a thin film at the water/organic phase interface via the Schotten–Baumann reaction [7]. The mechanism of interfacial polymerization continues to attract considerable research interest owing to the substantial use of PA-based TFC membranes for water purification; however, these highly cross-linked layers still suffer from extremely low permeability due to the low free volumes of the pure PA matrix [8–10]. Thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes, which are fabricated by incorporating nanomaterials, such as graphene oxide, metal/covalent organic frameworks, or aquaporins into the matrix, provide an ideal complement by enhancing water permeance while maintaining superior rejection without significant compromise [11]. Despite these advances, access to TFN membranes still involves complicated processing that may result in the formation of nonselective interfacial defects between the membrane matrix and the embedded nanofillers, owing to the incompatibility of the physical or/and surface chemical properties [12]. Other concerns about TFN membranes, including vulnerability under pressure-driven crossflow filtration and ambiguity in aqueous molecular diffusion/selectivity, still exists and hampers the development of highly-efficient nanofiltration, because the smaller kinetic pore diameters or/and non-polarity of the embedded nanofillers may also lead to the membrane performance failure [13]. To circumvent the above-mentioned challenges, new strategies that can directly employ the established versatility of the newly-developed nanomaterials and assemble them as building blocks for constructing the advanced TFC membranes with the custom-tailored functionalities or adsorption sites, which potentially takes over the conventional PA-based TFC/TFN membranes, are urgently needed [14]. Alongside this concept, a paradigm shift in engineering the surface active layer based on the new class of nanomaterials is thereby proposed.

Recently, the zero-dimensional material of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has attracted tremendous interest owing to the similarity of its chemical property to that of graphene but with smaller graphene flakes [15]. GQDs adopt a one-atom-thick graphene oxide (GO) nanosheet (<2 nm) structure, leading to a higher edge-to-volume ratio, and further possess the advantages of excellent chemical stability, low toxicity, and easy fabrication [16,17]. In view of the layered structures and porosity of GQDs, Bi and Wu et al. assembled them into TFC membranes, and they reported that the solvents (water or organic solvents) could be transported through the GQD-based layer with ultralow friction, similar to water molecules passing through the GO interlayers [18,19]. However, it should be noted that most of the resulting GQD-based TFC membranes still exhibited moderate selectivity merely used for dye separations with large molecular sizes, which is far less than those of regular PA-based membranes, or even 2D membranes stacked by dense nanosheets [20]. To the best of our knowledge, the GQD-based TFC membranes (purely assembled by GQDs) that can exhibit high selectivity toward ions and/or smaller neutral organic matters with molecular weights (Mw) less than 200 Da is rarely reported. Therefore, to further engineering the GQD-based TFC membranes with high exclusions while retaining the intrinsic merit of high permeating flux is still eagerly desired, to attain the full utilization of the unique channels of GQDs for the development of high-performance nanofiltration.

Herein, we demonstrate the fabrication of a highly permeable-selective GQD-based TFC nanofiltration membrane capable of remarkable precise separation via general and scalable interfacial polymerization process. As schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 , the intrinsically hydrophilic sulfonic groups are introduced to endow the membrane channels with stronger internal polarity and more accessible sites for easier water molecules infiltration. By rationally creating and tuning the molecular design of the amino/sulfonic decoration integrated with GQDs (GQDs_N/S) used as building blocks directly (not embedded in PA matrix), the assembled pore structures inside the membrane matrix could be regulated, from which an enhanced interparticle space with enlarged free volumes between GQDs is obtained for the rapid transport of water molecules inside the membrane, owing to the intercalation of sulfonic groups that can act as spacers to break up the tightly stacking structure of GQDs. Furthermore, the entirely assembled-GQDs_N/S connected by covalently molecular bridges between terminal amino groups and trimesoyl chloride enables the formation of a highly selective layer with amplified steric hindrance (molecular sieving effect) without interparticle defects, while the sulfonic groups maintaining a more robustly negative charge with stronger Donnan exclusion effect. Benefiting from the synergistic effects of the GQDs_N/S, the newly-developed TFC membrane exhibited a remarkably high water permeance up to 9.82 L m-2h−1 bar−1, and a favorable salt rejection of 97.4%, reaching more than 2-fold increase in water permeance without significantly sacrificing the membrane selectivity when compared to the mere amino decorated GQDs (GQDs_N) TFC membranes (with the water permeance of 4.57 L m-2h−1 bar−1, and Na2SO4 rejection ratio of 98.1%). To sum up, this work may provide an insightful guideline for the development of novel nanofiltration membranes with high perm-selectivity based on zero-dimensional materials, beyond GQDs based TFC membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Materials and chemicals
ce:section.ce:para: Polyethersulfone (PES) substrate with molecular separation of 5 kDa was purchased from Merck Millipore. Citric acid (CA, 99.5%), Tris(2-aminoethyl)amine (TAEA, 96%), 1, 3-Propanesultone (1, 3-PS, 99%), and 1, 3, 5-Benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC, 98%), Glycerol (99%), anhydrous D-(+)-Glucose (99.5%), Sucrose (99.5%), and D-(+)-Raffinose pentahydrate (98%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Inorganic salts of Na2SO4 (99%), MgSO4 (99%), CaCl2 (99%), and NaCl (99%) were purchased from FUJIFILM Wako Pure Chemical Co. (Osaka, Japan). Bovine serum albumin (BSA, 99%), humic acid (HA), sodium alginate (SA, 80 ~ 120 CP), and n-hexane (96%) was purchased from FUJIFILM Wako Pure Chemical Co. (Osaka, Japan). Pure water was supplied by a Milli-Q water purification system, produced by Merck Millipore.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), amino decorated GQDs (GQDs_N), and amino/sulfonic decorated GQDs (GQDs_N/S)
ce:section.ce:para: Pristine GQDs were synthesized via one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis of CA. Briefly, 10 ml of aqueous CA solution (5.0 mol/L) was prepared, followed by the microwave heating at 750 W for 200 s. The semi-finished products were subsequently cooled down to room temperature and dissolved in pure water for the dialysis for 3 days, of which the dialysis cassette was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Co. (2 K MWCO, Slide-A-Lyzer G2 Dialysis Cassettes). After that, the synthesized GQDs were reserved in pure water without drying for further use.

The GQDs_N and GQDs_N/S were fabricated via the same procedure with that of the pristine GQDs fabrication, except the additional mixture of TAEA (3.5 mol/L), or/and 1, 3-PS (0.4 mol/L, 0.8 mol/L, 1.6 mol/L, and 2.4 mol/L) into 10 ml of CA aqueous solution before the thermal treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of pristine GQDs TFC, GQDs_N TFC, and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The pristine GQDs TFC membranes were fabricated via interfacial polymerization method, as follows: 100 g aqueous solution containing GQDs (0.5 wt%) was prepared with the pretreatment of ultrasonication for 30 min. The obtained solution was then gently poured onto the surface of the PES substrate for 60 s. After draining off the excess aqueous solution, the 0.15 wt% TMC/n-hexane solution was rapidly covered the GQD-loaded substrate for another 45 s to form the selective layer. Then the heat-cured treatment was implemented for 3 min at 70 °C to increase the crosslinking degree of the polyamide network. The resultant GQDs TFC membranes were stored in pure water at 4 °C to finalize the hydrolysis of unreacted chloride groups from TMC.

The fabrication of GQDs_N TFC and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes were implemented via the same procedure with that of the GQDs TFC membranes fabrication, as the above described, except that the GQDs_N, or/and GQDs_N/S were added to the GQD-based aqueous solutions instead.

The GQDs_N/S TFC membranes fabricated from GQD-based materials with different sulfonic groups grafting (0.4 mol/L, 0.8 mol/L, 1.6 mol/L, and 2.4 mol/L), were designated as GQDs_N/S_0.4 TFC, GQDs_N/S_0.8 TFC, GQDs_N/S_1.6 TFC, and GQDs_N/S_2.4 TFC membranes, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization methods
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical compositions of pristine GQDs, GQDs_N, and GQDs_N/S were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Perkin-Elmer, Spectrum One System) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, JPS-9010 MC, JEOL, Japan). The crystalline structures of pristine GQDs, GQDs_N, and GQDs_N/S were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance Powder, Bruker) in the 2θ range of 10 ~ 50° with the intervals of 0.02°. The particle size/particle size distributions were of pristine GQDs, GQDs_N, and GQDs_N/S were measured by dynamic light scattering apparatus (DLS; ELSZ-1000ZA, Otsuka Electronics, Japan). Zeta potentials of each GQD-based aqueous colloidal suspension were measured at the pH value of 7 ± 0.5, of which this characterization was implemented by using the same DLS apparatus with that of particle sizes/particle size distributions characterization used. The pH value of each GQD-based aqueous colloidal suspension was measured using pH meter (F-51, Horiba. Co. Japan) before each measurement of zeta potentials. Surface microstructures of pristine GQDs, GQDs_N, and GQDs_N/S were examined by field-emission transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM, JEM 2100F, JEOL).

Field emission transmission electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JEM 2100F, JEOL) was used to observe the surface morphologies of GQD-based membranes, of which samples were sputter-coated with platinum to inhibit the charging effect. The atomic force microscopy (AFM, XE-100, Park System) characterization was carried out to evaluate the surface microstructures and roughness of GQD-based membranes. FE-TEM characterizations were conducted to observe the cross-sectional morphologies of GQD-based membranes. And the samples were prepared as follows: first, the samples were first entrapped into the resin, followed by drying at 60 °C for 48 h for solidification; then, the solidified resin was then cut into slides by using a microtome; third, the slides were picked up and placed onto the copper grid for further investigation. Water contact angles measurement was conducted to investigate the surface hydrophobicities of GQD-based membranes by using a goniometer (Drop Master 300, Kyowa Interface Science Co., Japan). The surface charge of the GQD-based membranes was investigated using a SurPASS streaming potential analyzer (Anton Paar GmbH, Austria) at 22 °C, under the background of 1 mM KCl solution. Free volumes of GQD-based membranes were measured via positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) at the Institution of High Energy Physics of Chinese Academy of Science, and the slow positron beams were from a 50 mCi (2005) Na source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanofiltration performance test of pristine GQDs TFC, GQDs_N TFC, and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance of the fabricated GQDs TFC, GQDs_N TFC, and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes were investigated via a lab-scale cross-flow filtration apparatus with an effective area of 14.5 cm2. The permeating flux was determined by measuring the weight change with respect to time, under the testing pressure of 10 bar, and the flowrate of 1 L·min−1. All the samples were pre-compacted by filtrating the Milli-Q DI water at 10 bar for 1 h until the stable permeating flux was achieved. The water permeability A (L m-2h−1 bar−1) was calculated by the following equation (1):
(1)
A =
Δ V
Δ P × Δ t × A
where
Δ V
is the permeate volume (L) during a collected time period (Δt; h);
Δ P
is the transmembrane pressure between the feed side and the permeate side (bar);
A
is the membrane effective surface area (m2).

The inorganic salt concentrations of feed solutions (Na2SO4, MgSO4, CaCl2, and NaCl) were 1000 ppm, of which the ion rejection ratios were calculated based on the electrical conductivity of the feed and the permeate. The salt rejection, R (%), was calculated using the following equation:

are the inorganic salt concentrations of feed and permeate solutions, respectively. The concentrations of inorganic salts were measured by using a conductivity meter (Ultrameter IITM 4P, Myron L Company, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) values, pore sizes, and pore size distributions of pristine GQDs TFC, GQDs_N TFC, and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The pore size, and pore size distributions of pristine GQDs TFC, GQDs_N TFC, and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes were determined by the rejection ratios of neutral organic compounds with different molecular weights, including glycerol (92 Da), glucose (180 Da), sucrose (342 Da), and raffinose (504 Da). The concentration of each organic compound was 1000 ppm, by using the same experimental condition with that of the rejection tests of inorganic salts (under the pressure of10 bar, and the flowrate of 1 L·min−1). The MWCO is the minimum molecular weight of the polymer at the rejection ratio of 90%. The pore size distribution curve is expressed as a probability density function that was established based on the following assumptions: 1). There are no steric or hydrodynamic interactions between these organic solutes and the membrane pores; 2). The mean pore size of the polyamide equals the Stokes radius of the organic solute with a measured rejection of 50%; 3). The distribution of membrane pore size is characterized by the geometric standard deviation of the probability density function curve, which is the ratio between the Stokes radius at a rejection ratio of 84.13% to that at a rejection ratio of 50%, as follows:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling tests of GQDs_N TFC, and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
BSA, HA, and SA solutions were selected to investigate the fouling-resistant and fouling-releasing properties of GQDs_N TFC, and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes. All the membranes were first pre-filtrated with pure water under a transmembrane pressure of 10 bar, under a flow rate of 1 L·min−1 for 1 h to reach a steady state, where the water flux was denoted as J0 (L·m−2·h−1). The feed solution was then changed to 1 g·L-1 solution of BSA/HA/SA containing 0.2 g·L-1 of CaCl2 for subsequent 1-h filtration under the same operation condition with that of the pure water filtration tests, of which period the permeating flux was denoted as J1 (L·m−2·h−1). After this, the membranes were then filtrated with pure water for another 1 h to obtain steady water permeating flux denoted as J2 (L·m−2·h−1). The fouling ratio (Fr) and the flux recovery ratio (Frr) were evaluated using the following equations:

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-term stability tests of GQDs_N TFC, and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The continuous long-term stability tests of GQDs_N TFC, and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes were conducted under the same experimental condition with that of the pure water filtration tests, excepted filtration time was changed to the prolonged testing time of 12 h for the pure water filtration, followed by the 6 h for the aqueous Na2SO4 solutions filtration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational simulation
ce:section.ce:para: Molecular dynamics simulation. The molecular models used in this simulation were all modeled with the commercial software BIOVIA Materials Studio® 2018, and molecular dynamics simulations were also conducted using the same software. The Condensed-phase Optimized Molecular Potential for Atomistic Simulation Studies II (COMPASS II) force field was used for energy minimization and the MD simulations [21–23]. Three GQD models with different functional groups were prepared. The structure of water molecules around the GQD was investigated by the radial distribution function (RDF) [24]. The effect of functional groups on the interaction between water molecules and the GQD was studied through the analysis of the interaction energy and hydrogen bond formation. In addition, the effect of the structures with the different functional groups was investigated through the analysis of the free volume.

Modeling of GQD. Three GQD models employed in this study are shown in Fig. S1. The size of three GQD was about 1.6 nm in length and about 1.0 nm in width. The area of three GQD-based models was approximately 1.6 nm2. Fig. 12(a) is a pristine GQD model having two carboxyl groups, two hydroxyl groups, and two ether groups, and Fig. 12(b) is a GQDs_N model obtained by replacing two hydrogen atoms by two amino groups; and Fig. 12(c) is a GQDs_N/S model obtained by further replacing two hydrogen atoms by two sulfonic groups.

Water-GQD simulation cell. 35 Å × 35 Å × 35 Å simulation cell in size with periodic boundaries in all directions was prepared. In the cell, 1430 water molecules were included to model an aqueous phase with a density of 1.0 g/cm3. The system, including one GQD in the initial position in the center of the cell in this aqueous phase, was modeled with pristine GQDs, GQDs_N, and GQDs_N/S, respectively. After structural optimization of these cells, MD simulations were performed under the condition of constant N(the number of molecules), V(volume), and T(temperature)(NVT ensemble) for 2.0 ns at 298 K.

GQD-TMC simulation system. GQD-TMC system consisting of twelve GQDs and four TMC molecules were modeled in order to analyze the internal void structures of TFC membranes. GQD-TMC system was composed of one TMC and three GQDs_N, or GQDs_N/S.

The GQD-TMC system was constructed using the two GQD-TMC models in Fig. S2. Initial density was modeled as 1.37 g/cm3 with reference to the density of an actual polyamide membrane, and the cell size was 22.32 Å × 22.32 Å × 22.32 Å [25]. After structural optimization of this cell, MD calculation was conducted under the condition of constant N (the number of molecules), P (pressure), and T (temperature). The set pressure of 0.01, 0.1, 1.0 GPa was employed in the calculation for 300 ps at 298 K.

The most stable structures were obtained under 0.1GPa for the GQDs_N_TMC system and 0.01GPa for GQDs_N/S_TMC system, respectively. The optimized density was 1.421 g/cm3 for the GQDs_N_TMC system and 1.445 g/cm3 for GQDs_N/S_TMC system. GQDs_N_TMC and GQDs_N/S_TMC systems were 22 Å × 22 Å × 22 Å and 23.36 Å × 23.36 Å × 23.36 Å, respectively. MD calculation was conducted under the NVT ensemble for 300 ps at 298 K. The free volume was calculated as based on the Connolly surface with a probe radius of 1.5 Å. The simulation period from 0.5 ns to 1.0 ns was used for the calculation of the average free volume.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Physico-chemical characterizations of the pristine GQDs, GQDs_N, and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Three GQD-based nanofillers, including pristine GQDs, GQDs_N, and GQDs_N/S were synthesized, and the GQDs were used as control samples to investigate the surface functionalization of the introduced amino (–NH2) and sulfonic (-SO3H) groups. According to the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 2 (a)), all three GQD-based nanoparticles exhibited similar patterns, and their main peaks were located at 1175, 1700, and 3200 cm−1, which correspond to the vibrations of C = C/C–C, C-O, C = O, and C-OH from the hybridized carbon ring and carboxyl groups, respectively [26]. For the newly developed GQDs_N and GQDs_N/S, new scissoring bands located at 860 and 760 cm−1 emerged, which correspond to the –NH/–NH2 and S-O/S = O groups, respectively, indicating the presence and wide variation of the hydrophilic groups [27,28]. Moreover, both the GQDs_N and the GQDs_N/S revealed intensified peaks of N 1 s at 401 eV and S 2p at 167 eV, as shown in Fig. 2(b) when compared to those of the pristine GQDs, indicating successful surface functionalization via the introduction of tris(2-aminoethyl)amine (TAEA) and 1, 3-propanesultone (1,3-PS) [29]. According to the results shown in Fig. S3, both the GQD_N and the GQD_N/S manifested a stronger C-N peak intensity than that of the pristine GQDs, further ensuring the presence of the –NH2/–NH groups that would be responsible for interfacial polymerization (Fig. S3(a) and (b)) [30]. Furthermore, the new S 2p peaks (Fig. S3(c)) primarily belong to the sulfonic groups for GQD_N/S nanoparticles [31,32]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the pristine GQDs, GQD_N, and GQD_N/S were similar, as shown in Fig. 2(c), where all their spectra presented a crystalline band at ~ 19°, suggesting that their crystalline structures were nearly unaffected by the surface amino/sulfonic functionalization. According to the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 2(d), (e), and (f)), the pristine GQDs showed typical zero-dimensional nanostructures with a primary particle size of ~ 5 nm (Fig. 2(d)), while the larger GQDs_N and GQDs_N/S had primary particle sizes of 10 to 20 nm. These results agree well with the measurements from the dynamic light scattering (DLS) apparatus (shown in Fig. S4). Therefore, the incorporation of the TAEA and 1,3-PS led to more extensive aggregates and precipitates of GQDs_N and GQDs_N/S, probably due to the enhanced surface tension and interparticle interactions [33]. Fig. S5 exhibits the values of the GQD-based surface charges of the colloidal suspensions; the pristine GQDs exhibited relatively low values (-6.74 mV) because of the negatively charged carboxylic groups from the pyrolysis of the citric acid source. The GQDs_N displayed the highest value (7.33 mV) due to the presence of amino groups derived from the TAEA. In contrast, the sulfonic/sulfonate groups decorated GQDs (GQDs_S) and GQDs_N/S had the lowest values (-14.11 and −8.93 mV, respectively), owing to the negatively charged sulfonic/sulfonate groups derived from the 1,3-PS. These results suggest that the surface charge property of the GQD-based membranes could be tuned by adjusting the density of the surface sulfonic/sulfonate groups.

Interfacial polymerizations of the pristine GQDs, GQDs_N, and GQDs_N/S with TMC were conducted on polyethersulfone substrates to prepare the GQD-based TFC membranes. According to the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) characterization results (Fig. 3 (a), the nodular structures observed on the GQDs_N and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes were more prominent when compared to the pristine GQDs TFC membrane, which had a relatively smoother surface. The corrugated structure evolved into aggregates of the GQDs_N and GQDs_N/S nanofillers after the surface amino modification, suggesting the successful formation of a defect-free thin film selective layer covered the entire polyethersulfone (PES) substrate (Fig. S6) [34]. The intrinsic thickness of the selective layers was measured using cross-sectional TEM images (Fig. 3(b)), whereof it can be observed that the pristine GQDs TFC membrane exhibited a less continuous nanoparticle-assembled structure than those of the more tightly packed GQDs_N and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes with a selective layer thickness of ~ 55 nm. Further evidence was provided by atomic force microscopy (AFM) analyses (Fig. 3(c)), which revealed that both GQDs_N and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes had enhanced average surface roughness (Ra) of ~ 14.8 and ~ 14.0 nm, respectively, in contrast to that of the pristine GQDs TFC membrane with a relatively smoother Ra of ~ 6.3 nm, matching well with the SEM characterization results. The emergence of the more prominent nodular profiles of the GQDs_N and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes should be primarily attributed to the amplified crosslinking degree via PA-based intramolecular interactions. Owing to the rapid reaction kinetics, the unevenly distributed GQDs_N and GQDs_N/S nanofillers on the substrate surfaces were in-situ assembled into an intact film upon cross-linking by TMC, leading to the formation of ridged surface microstructures [35,36]. It should be noted that the enhanced surface roughness potentially corresponds to a larger effective filtration area, which is conducive to elevate water permeance [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation properties of the GQDs_N and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Cross-flow, high-pressure (up to 10 bar), and continuous filtration processes were conducted to investigate the separation properties of all the GQD-based TFC membranes, as shown in Fig. 4 . According to the results shown in Fig. 4(a) and (b), the GQDs_N/S TFC membrane exhibited a superior water permeance of 9.82 L m-2h−1 bar−1, reaching nearly 115% higher than that of the GQD_N TFC membrane (4.57 L m-2h−1 bar−1) without a significant compromise in desalination performance (the Na2SO4 rejection ratio of GQDs_N/S TFC membrane remained at 97.4% when compared to that of GQD_N TFC membrane of 98.1%). This indicates that the presence of sulfonic groups in the membrane matrix substantially enhances membrane permeability. As shown in Fig. 4(b), both the GQDs_N and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes demonstrated the selectivity to inorganic salts in the sequence of Na2SO4 > MgSO4 > CaCl2 > NaCl. This result conforms to the Donnan effect that the more negatively charged GQDs_N/S over the GQDs_N TFC membranes at the pH value of 7 [27], as shown in Fig. S7. The salt rejection ratio is primarily determined by electrostatic repulsive and/or attractive interactions between the ions and the membrane surface. While the surface charge of the membrane surfaces played a crucial role in inorganic ions exclusion, the stronger electrostatic repulsion against the higher-valence divalent salt should be mainly explained by the introduction of sulfonic groups with strengthened negatively charged properties [38]. Furthermore, it should be noted that the pristine GQDs TFC membrane had the highest water permeance (31.3 L m-2h−1 bar−1) with the lowest desalination (salt rejection ratios of less than 30%). This can likely be ascribed to the formed defects, owing to the weak interactions between the pristine GQDs because the covalent interfacial bonding cannot be reached without the terminal amino groups reacting with the acyl chloride groups of the TMC [20 39].

The higher water permeances achieved by the GQDs_N/S TFC membranes should be mainly attributed to the enlarged pore size of the GQD-based selective layer, which was confirmed by the rejection of neutral organic molecules with different molecular weights, as shown in Fig. 4(c). As shown in Fig. 4(c), the GQDs_N TFC membrane exhibited a broad pore size distribution with an effective mean pore size of 3.8 Å, while the GQDs_N/S TFC membrane displayed a sharp pore size distribution with a larger effective mean pore size of 5.1 Å. Sharp pore size distributions generally correspond to the sharp cutoff property with more uniform sub-nanometer pores [4]. This suggests that the sulfonic modification exerted a restricting effect on interfacial polymerization in sharpening the pore size distribution with a more sophisticated separating efficiency instead of merely shifting the pore size/pore size distribution to a larger range. Positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) characterization was performed to determine the free volume of the selective layer of the GQD-based TFC membranes. The free volume of the GQD-based TFC membranes was positively correlated with the lifetime of o-Ps annihilation (τ 3) in polymers, and the depth profile of the free volume properties alongside the TFC membranes could be accessed by controlling the position incident energy [40]. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the scans of the GQDs_N and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes both revealed S parameters in the 0.2–5 keV positron incident energy range, which corresponds to a depth of ~ 500 nm in the TFC membranes. The S parameters could be divided into three major zones: the top GQD-based layer (Zone I), the interfacial region (Zone II), and the PES substrate (Zone III). The S parameters generally increased with increasing position incident energy, indicating gradually enlarged free volume cavities in the GQDs_N and GQDs_N/S TFC membranes from the tightly selective GQD-based layer (Zone I) down to the highly porous PES substrates (Zone III) [18], as demonstrated in the TEM images in Fig. 3(b). It should be noted that the distribution of the S parameter for the GQDs_N TFC membrane was lower than that of the GQDs_N/S TFC membrane, indicating a smaller free volume cavity [41]. From the PALS measurements and the separation of neutral organic molecules, it can be concluded that the sulfonic modification acting as spacers contributed to GQD-based TFC membrane microstructures with a relatively loose porous structure and larger free volumes. In addition, the enhanced water permeance of the GQDs_N/S TFC membrane can also be explained by the improved surface hydrophilicity (which had the lowest water contact angle (CA) of ~ 20° of GQDs_N/S TFC membrane, compared to ~ 40° and ~ 65° for the GQDs_N and pristine GQDs TFC membranes, respectively), as shown in Fig. S8, owing to the incorporation of highly hydrophilic sulfonic groups. This enhanced internal polarity (hydrophilicity) tends to prompt the water molecules to easily infiltrate into the membrane matrix with enlarged free volume cavities for quick transport through the GQDs_N/S TFC membrane [8].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism discussion on the high-performance separation of the GQDs_N/S TFC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: A molecular dynamics (MD) simulation was implemented to uncover the intramolecular interactions between the water molecules and pristine GQDs, GQDs_N, and GQDs_N/S, as shown in Fig. S9. According to the results shown in Fig. S9(a) (the simulated system of GQDN/S inside a water tank), the water molecules adhered to the GQDs_N/S surfaces via hydrogen bonds (H-bonds), connecting to the carboxylic (–COOH), amino (–NH2), and sulfonic groups. This indicates that the enhanced internal polarity of the GQDs_N/S was primarily derived from the abundance of hydrophilic groups grafted onto the surfaces [42]. Fig. S9(b) shows that the GQDs_N/S exhibited the strongest interaction energy (-737 kCal mol−1) with water molecules and possessed the highest H-bond number (20), suggesting that it had the highest internal polarity with the best surface hydrophilicity [43]. In contrast, the GQDs_N and pristine GQDs had relatively moderate hydrophilic properties because of their relatively lower interaction energies (-601 and −540 kCal mol−1, respectively) and lower H-bonds numbers (16 and 14, respectively). To further investigate the underlying cause of the strongly cohesive forces with water molecules displayed by the GQDs_N/S, a radial distribution function (RDF) analysis was conducted, as shown in Fig. S9(c) and (d). According to the results shown in Fig. S9(c), the highest intensity peak of the GQDs_N/S was located at a closer intramolecular distance between the oxygen atoms of the water molecules (~1.7 Å) when compared to the characteristic peaks of the GQDs_N and pristine GQDs. This can be primarily ascribed to the interatomic structures the oxygen atom (O(H2O)) formed with the sulfur atom (S(-SO3H)), signaling a higher propensity toward water molecules than those of the nitrogen atom in amino (N(–NH2)) and the oxygen atom in the carboxylic group (O(–COOH)), as shown in Fig. S9(d). These results indicate that a more hydrophilic GQD-based TFC membrane with a stronger preferential water-capturing property could be obtained by using the GQDs_N/S as building blocks, owing to its superior interaction with water molecules.

To further investigate the mechanism that caused the superior performance of the GQDs_N/S TFC over the GQDs_N TFC membrane, crosslinked GQD-based networks were constructed to simulate the reaction between the TMC and the GQDs_N and/or GQDs_N/S molecules, and the internal voids (free volumes) were analyzed, as shown in Fig. 5 . GQDs_N/S_TMC network exhibited larger internal voids (1.774 nm3, marked with the gray color) with more accessible entrances (marked with the blue color) for water molecules [44], reaching a nearly two-fold increase in the size of the internal voids compared with that of the GQDs_N_TMC network (0.987 nm3). The MD simulation results match well with those obtained from the PALS characterization (shown in Fig. 4(d)). The enlarged free volumes of the GQDs_N/S TFC membranes may derive from two aspects: 1) The adsorbed sulfonic groups with stronger steric repulsion effects against the diffusion of TMC reacted with the –NH2. Because of the increased diffusion resistance caused by the sulfonic groups, the polymerization reaction was retarded, forming the relatively loose structure of the GQDs_N/S_TMC network that enhanced the water permeating flux while deteriorating the salt rejection capability [27,45]. 2). More water molecules tended to be absorbed by the GQDs_N/S due to the stronger internal polarity, and the water molecules preferentially reacted with the TMC, hydrolyzing the acyl chloride into carboxyl acids and acting as terminators to interrupt the normal interfacial polymerization reaction. This led to the formation of more internal voids in the vicinity of the GQDs_N/S, which is beneficial for the rapid transport of water molecules [46,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: General strategy and long-term filtration stability of the GQDs_N/S TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of the sulfonic group in promoting the separation properties of the GQDs_N/S TFC membranes was systematically investigated, as shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b). The water permeance increased from 4.82 L m-2h−1 bar−1 for the newly developed GQDs_N/S TFC membrane with the addition of merely 0.4 mol L-1 of 1,3-PS, to 9.82 L m-2h−1 bar−1 for the GQDs_N/S TFC membrane with the addition of 1.6 mol L-1 of 1,3-PS, while the Na2SO4 rejection ratio was not significantly compromised (remaining at 97.4%). However, while increasing the added mass of the sulfonic group to 2.4 mol L-1, the water permeance enhanced to 13.27 L m-2h−1 bar−1; nevertheless, the rejection ratio toward Na2SO4 was drastically reduced to 82.7%. This indicates that the grafting of the sulfonic groups onto the GQDs_N/S played a significant role in the membrane separation property, and the higher concentration of 1,3-PS may lead to the formation of loose self-assembled pore structures with a lower degree of crosslinking. As summarized in Table S1, the crosslinking degree of the GQDs_N/S TFC membranes deteriorated from 76.49% to 42.91% when the added mass of 1,3-PS increased from 0.4 to 2.4 mol L-1. Therefore, a balance is required between the membrane permeability and selectivity to achieve the elevated nanofiltration separation, and the optimum adding mass of 1,3-PS addition of 1.6 mol L-1 was thereby selected for further study. This is because of its superior separation property in the combination of the relatively high water permeance and acceptable salt rejection ratio. Furthermore, the enhanced water permeance of the GQDs_N/S TFC membranes could also be partially attributed to the improved surface hydrophilic properties (Fig. S10) caused by the introduction of more sulfonic groups (showing stronger sulfur intensities) with amplified internal polarity, as exhibited in Fig. S11. In addition, it should be noted that all the GQDs_N/S TFC membranes exhibited similar desalination properties, which followed the sequence of rejection ratios of Na2SO4 > MgSO4 > CaCl2 > NaCl, as shown in Fig. 6(b). This could be primarily explained by Donnan exclusion, in which the GQDs_N/S TFC membranes manifested a stronger electrostatic repulsion against the solute anions (SO4 2- and Cl-) than the cations (Mg2+, Ca2+, and Na+), owing to the more negatively charged GQDs_N/S TFC membrane surfaces with larger numbers of grafted negatively charged sulfonic groups [38], as shown in Fig. S12.

Despite the superior separation performance of the GQDs_N/S TFC membrane, concerns still remained that they might be delaminated owing to the strong shear force over the long-term filtration process. The long-term stability of the membrane separation property was tested, as shown in Fig. 7 (a). The newly developed GQDs_N TFC membrane exhibited a nearly constant water permeance of ~ 4.5 L m-2h−1 bar−1 for the first 12 h of continuous filtration of pure water, and maintained a comparably high rejection ratio of 97.7% over the subsequent 6-h filtration of an aqueous Na2SO4 solution. However, the water permeance of the GQDs_N/S TFC membrane (~9.4 L m-2h−1 bar−1) remained nearly 110% higher than that of the pristine GQDs_N TFC membrane, without a prominent deterioration in the rejection ratio against Na2SO4 (97.2%), over the whole filtration test. This demonstrates the robust structural stability of the GQD-based TFC membranes, which could be attributed to the PA-based covalent interparticle bridging between the terminal –NH/–NH2 groups and the acyl chloride of the TMC [39,48]. For the very slight change of the membrane states during the whole 18-h filtration tests, whereof the water permeance of GQDs_N/S TFC membrane slightly decreased from 7.3 to 7.0 L m-2h−1 bar−1, this could be likely attributed to the membrane compaction that leads to the formation of the denser matrix with higher intrinsic resistance for water molecules transport [8,43].

As demonstrated in Fig. 7(b), from which all the specific data of each membrane are summarized in Table S2, the GQDs_N/S TFC membrane exhibited superior competitiveness when compared to extensive state-of-the-art NF membranes constituting various materials, such as GO/reduced GO [49], carbon nanotubes [50], GQDs [15,20,51], inorganics [52,53], polyelectrolytes [54–56], and other polymer-based TFN membranes [5,6,11,26,27]. The preferential separation property, in terms of high water permeance and high salt rejection ratio, should be attributed to the intrinsically hydrophilic GQDs_N/S building blocks that endowed the TFC membrane with enhanced internal polarity for water molecules to quickly transport through the interconnected nanochannels constituted from the self-assembled GQDs_N/S with enlarged free volumes.

Fig. S13 exhibits the fouling-resistant and fouling-releasing properties of the GQD-based TFC membranes against bovine serum albumin (BSA), humic acid (HA), and sodium alginate (SA). Both BSA and SA were selected to exemplify the higher molecular weight compounds present in wastewater, and HA was chosen to demonstrate the effect of hydrophobic organic matter on the membrane fouling propensity. The GQDs_N/S TFC membrane exhibited a superior normal permeating flux to that of the GQDs_N TFC membrane with all foulants, suggesting that the surface modification with 1,3-PS substantially improved the antifouling property. This could be primarily ascribed to the introduction of sulfonic groups with enhanced hydrophilicity (Fig. S8), which can efficiently prevent the foulants from directly contacting the membranes, owing to the absorbed hydration shell that tends to act as a kinetic barrier with ultralow adhesion forces [57,58]. Furthermore, the more robust negatively charged membrane surface led to the augmentation of the electrostatic repulsion against BSA with the same negatively charged property. The GQDs_N TFC membrane suffered from more severe surface fouling, partially owing to the strong interactions between the relatively more hydrophobic surface and the organic compounds [59]. It should be noted that a more severe decrease in the permeating flux was obtained for both TFC membranes when they were treated by SA than by that of the BSA and HA solutions. This could be ascribed to the bridging effect between the Ca2+ and carboxylic groups of SA, which results in the formation of highly dense cake-fouling and severe pore-blocking [60]. This further demonstrates that the GQDs_N/S TFC membrane is a promising candidate for promoting water remediation with robustly long-term and superior antifouling properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, nanoparticle-assembled highly permeable-selective GQDs_N/S TFC membranes were successfully developed via interfacial polymerization, and intriguing molecular design for the interface-decoration of amino/sulfonic groups on GQDs was achieved. The synergistic effects of the amino/sulfonic groups endowed the TFC membrane with simultaneous enhancements in water permeance and selectivity toward ions or small molecular solutes (Mw less than 200 Da). This should be attributed to the magnified pore channels with enlarged free volume cavities, owing to the intercalation of sulfonic groups acting as spacers to break up the tightly stacking structure of GQDs_N/S. Furthermore, the introduced sulfonic groups also endowed the membrane with stronger internal polarity and more accessible sites for easier water molecules infiltration, while GQDs_N/S are covalently immobilized via the interactions between the terminal amino groups and trimesoyl chloride. As a result, the GQDs_N/S TFC membrane exhibited superior separation properties in the combination of remarkably high water permeance up to 9.82 L m-2h−1 bar−1 and a compelling rejection of 97.4% against Na2SO4. Further, it exhibited outstanding antifouling properties and durability under harsh operating conditions (cross-flow, high-pressure, and continuously long-term filtration). In summary, this work opens new opportunities for closely packed GQD-based TFC membranes for high-performance nanofiltration.
